prompt: What should the default governance posture be when a repo adopts a central config pointer?
choices: merge — local config may override central policy (backwards-compatible; central control is opt-in via `strict`) [recommended]
strict — central pointer is authoritative; no local policy override for governed checks (safer org-wide, but a breaking change for existing consumers)
allowlisted-local-override — strict, except an explicit declared allowlist of locally-overridable checks/fields

prompt: Should the Shadow gate decision (run-but-don't-enforce; surface findings as info/record-only) be in scope for v1? It is the highest-value state for fleet ramp but adds an enforcement/observation split to the runner plus a new output field.
When the experiment/gating service is unreachable (e.g. CI offline), how should a CheckGate behave?
choices: Fail to config-default — gate yields the statically configured enabled/severity; run proceeds deterministically [recommended]
Fail-closed — treat affected checks as Disabled
Fail-open — treat affected checks as Enabled (enforce)

prompt: What trust posture should declarative (host-binary-executing) checks from a non-first-party bundle have?
choices: Off by default; explicit per-bundle opt-in, invocations constrained to the bundle's declared `needs` [recommended]
Allowed but constrained to an explicit allowlist of permitted binaries per repo
Forbidden entirely for third-party bundles (only first-party bundled declarative checks run)

prompt: Which transport should the shared versioned-pointer substrate use for v1?
choices: HTTPS + sha256 — reuses existing reqwest fetch + retry/backoff and the .cwasm-cache patterns [recommended]
Git-repo-ref — resolve a git ref to a commit and fetch a path
